report_metadata.cancer_type: Parotid Acinic Cell Carcinoma with High-Grade Transformation
molecular_profile: NR4A3 rearrangement (confirmatory for acinic cell carcinoma origin)
Ki-67 proliferative index 40% (consistent with high-grade transformation)
Prior parotid surgery 8 years ago (scarred operative field)

patient_demographics.relevant_history: Prior parotid surgery 8 years ago; recurrent/transformed disease in previously operated field

treatments.name: Radical Revision Parotidectomy with Facial Nerve Sacrifice and Ipsilateral Neck Dissection

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN, ASCO, and ESMO guidelines uniformly recommend complete surgical resection as primary treatment for salivary gland malignancies. For T3N1 high-grade tumors, total parotidectomy with sacrifice of involved facial nerve branches and formal neck dissection (levels I-V or selective levels I-III) is standard. Evidence from large retrospective series (REFCOR study, n=187; PMC4838973, n=25 HGT cases) consistently supports aggressive surgery.

survival_benefit.rationale: Surgery is the cornerstone of curative intent. In the PMC4838973 series, 3/25 (12%) HGT patients achieved long-term disease-free survival (mean 7.3 years), all through complete surgical excision. 5-year OS after salvage parotidectomy for recurrent disease reported at 66.7% in one series. However, 86% of HGT patients recur despite aggressive surgery, and median survival remains 2.2 years for HGT-AcCC.

accessibility.rationale: Widely available at head and neck surgical centers. Re-operation in scarred field requires experienced head and neck surgeon, ideally at high-volume center with microsurgical capability for nerve grafting. Simultaneously available at most academic medical centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Re-operation in previously operated parotid field carries increased risk: distorted anatomy, fibrosis, higher facial nerve injury rate, increased bleeding risk, wound complications, Frey syndrome. Facial nerve sacrifice results in complete ipsilateral facial paralysis (House-Brackmann VI). Additional morbidity from neck dissection includes shoulder dysfunction, lymphedema, chyle leak.

biomarker_match.rationale: NR4A3 rearrangement confirms acinic cell origin, supporting the diagnosis of high-grade transformation rather than a new primary. This diagnostic confirmation justifies the aggressive surgical approach appropriate for HGT-AcCC. Ki-67 40% confirms high-grade biology necessitating radical rather than conservative resection.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete macroscopic and microscopic tumor removal through radical revision total parotidectomy with sacrifice of the facial nerve (given direct nerve involvement/perineural invasion), followed by ipsilateral comprehensive neck dissection for N1 nodal disease. Re-operation in a scarred field requires meticulous dissection to identify tissue planes altered by prior surgery.

key_evidence.study_name: Clinicopathologic characterization of 25 cases of AcCC with high-grade transformation (Stanley et al.)

biomarker_requirements: NR4A3 rearrangement confirms acinic cell carcinoma origin
Ki-67 index and histomorphology confirm high-grade transformation
notable_side_effects: Complete ipsilateral facial paralysis (House-Brackmann VI) from nerve sacrifice
Increased wound complications in scarred/re-operated field
Risk of flap necrosis and salivary fistula
Shoulder dysfunction from neck dissection (accessory nerve preservation attempted)
treatments.availability: Standard of Care — available at head and neck surgery centers worldwide

treatments.name: Adjuvant Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT) — 60-66 Gy to Parotid Bed and Neck

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN/ASCO/ESMO guidelines strongly recommend adjuvant radiation therapy for high-grade salivary gland tumors, positive/close margins, T3-T4 tumors, nodal disease, and perineural invasion — all of which apply to this patient. ASCO guidelines recommend at least 60 Gy to high-risk targets. National Cancer Database analysis showed improved OS with adjuvant RT (HR 0.76, p=0.002).

survival_benefit.rationale: Adjuvant RT significantly improves locoregional control (89.1% at 5 years per PMC10621453). Five-year OS with adjuvant RT was 93.8% in one major series. In the REFCOR study of 187 acinic cell carcinoma patients, adjuvant RT improved relapse-free survival. For HGT specifically, RT did not show detectable impact on systemic recurrence but improves local control in a disease where local failure contributes substantially to morbidity.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT is widely available at radiation oncology centers. Proton therapy, which offers dosimetric advantages for parotid bed irradiation near the brainstem and temporal lobe, is available at a growing number but still limited number of centers. Standard photon-based IMRT is universally accessible.

safety_profile.rationale: Expected toxicities include mucositis (grade 1-2 in majority), xerostomia from contralateral parotid/submandibular dose, skin dermatitis, potential osteoradionecrosis of mandible, fibrosis in previously operated field, hearing changes, dental complications. Late effects include trismus and chronic dry mouth. Grade 3+ toxicity rate is low (~3%) with modern IMRT planning.

biomarker_match.rationale: High Ki-67 (40%) suggests radiosensitive rapidly proliferating tumor population that may be more susceptible to radiation-induced DNA damage. High-grade transformation with perineural invasion is a specific guideline-based indication for adjuvant RT regardless of margin status.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-energy photon or proton radiation delivered to the parotid bed, ipsilateral neck nodal levels, and potentially contralateral level II in 30-33 daily fractions. IMRT technique allows dose sculpting to spare contralateral parotid gland, brainstem, temporal lobes, cochlea, and mandible while delivering 60-66 Gy to high-risk clinical target volumes. Radiation induces lethal DNA double-strand breaks in residual microscopic tumor cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Adjuvant Radiotherapy for Major Salivary Gland Malignancies: Impact on Locoregional Control and Survival (Stuppner et al.)

biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirements — indicated by clinical/pathological features
High-grade histology, T3 stage, N1 disease, and perineural invasion are sufficient indications
treatments.availability: Standard of Care — IMRT universally available; proton therapy at specialized centers

treatments.name: Proton Beam Radiation Therapy (Adjuvant or Definitive)
treatments.category: Standard of Care — Advanced Radiation Technology

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II/retrospective data supports proton therapy for salivary gland malignancies, showing favorable local control with reduced late toxicity compared to photon IMRT. Johns Hopkins and MD Anderson have published institutional series. No randomized trials comparing proton vs photon RT for salivary gland cancer exist, but dosimetric superiority is well-established for reducing dose to brainstem, temporal lobes, and contralateral structures.

survival_benefit.rationale: Equivalent or superior local control rates compared to photon IMRT (reported >85% 3-year locoregional control). Main advantage is reduced toxicity profile rather than improved tumor control. For re-irradiation scenarios (if this patient had prior RT), proton therapy offers particular advantage in limiting cumulative dose to previously treated normal tissues.

accessibility.rationale: Proton therapy centers remain limited in number (approximately 45 centers in the US as of 2025). Requires travel for most patients. Cost significantly higher than photon IMRT. Insurance coverage can require prior authorization and appeal processes, though most major payers now cover proton therapy for head and neck cancers.

safety_profile.rationale: Superior normal tissue sparing compared to photon IMRT: reduced dose to contralateral parotid (preserving remaining salivary function), lower brainstem and temporal lobe doses, reduced integral dose. In the re-operation setting with scarred tissues, the sharper dose fall-off may reduce wound healing complications. Acute toxicity profile is similar to IMRT.

biomarker_match.rationale: No specific biomarker predicts proton therapy response. The advantage is physical (dose distribution) rather than biological. However, the high Ki-67 (40%) suggests a radiosensitive tumor, and the ability to safely deliver higher doses (up to 70 Gy with protons) may exploit this sensitivity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beams deposit energy in a characteristic Bragg peak, allowing precise dose deposition at the target depth with minimal exit dose beyond the tumor. Intensity-modulated proton therapy (IMPT) enables conformal treatment plans that spare brainstem, temporal lobes, cochlea, contralateral parotid, mandible, and oral cavity while delivering tumoricidal doses (60-70 Gy RBE) to the parotid bed and regional lymphatics.

key_evidence.study_name: Proton Therapy for Major Salivary Gland Cancer: Clinical Outcomes

biomarker_requirements: Treatment planning based on anatomic tumor location and proximity to critical structures
notable_side_effects: Reduced xerostomia compared to photon IMRT
Lower risk of temporal lobe necrosis
treatments.availability: Available at approximately 45 proton therapy centers in the US; limited global availability

treatments.name: Entrectinib or Larotrectinib (TRK Inhibitors — if NTRK fusion present)

evidence_level.rationale: Both entrectinib and larotrectinib have FDA approval for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors regardless of histology. Pan-tumor approval based on phase I/II basket trials showing >70% ORR. However, NTRK fusions are extremely rare in acinic cell carcinoma (which characteristically has NR4A3 rearrangement, not NTRK fusions). Secretory carcinoma of salivary gland, not acinic cell, is the NTRK-driven salivary malignancy.

survival_benefit.rationale: If NTRK fusion-positive, ORR >75% with durable responses. Median duration of response >12 months. Transformative for the rare patient with this molecular alteration. However, the probability of finding an NTRK fusion in NR4A3-rearranged acinic cell carcinoma is extremely low, making this largely a theoretical consideration.

accessibility.rationale: Both drugs are FDA-approved for NTRK fusion-positive tumors. Widely available at oncology centers. Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation Medicine, Tempus, etc.) can identify NTRK fusions. Cost is high (~$20,000/month) but covered by insurance with confirmed NTRK fusion.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated oral agents. Main toxicities: dizziness, fatigue, constipation, dysgeusia (entrectinib); weight gain, fatigue, nausea (larotrectinib). Grade 3+ toxicities in <10% of patients. No significant overlapping toxicity with surgery or radiation.

biomarker_match.rationale: NTRK fusion is the required biomarker. NR4A3 rearrangement-positive acinic cell carcinoma is extremely unlikely to harbor concurrent NTRK fusions, as these are different oncogenic drivers. Comprehensive genomic profiling should still be performed to exclude this possibility, but probability is very low.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Larotrectinib and entrectinib are selective inhibitors of tropomyosin receptor kinases (TRKA, TRKB, TRKC encoded by NTRK1/2/3 genes). NTRK gene fusions create constitutively active chimeric TRK proteins that drive tumor cell proliferation and survival. TRK inhibitors block this aberrant signaling, inducing tumor regression. Entrectinib additionally inhibits ROS1 and ALK.

key_evidence.study_name: Larotrectinib in patients with TRK fusion-positive tumours (integrated analysis)

biomarker_requirements: Comprehensive genomic profiling or FISH testing required
Extremely rare in NR4A3-rearranged acinic cell carcinoma
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for NTRK fusion-positive tumors — requires molecular confirmation

treatments.name: Lenvatinib plus Pembrolizumab Combination

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II data presented at ASCO 2025 showed remarkable activity specifically in acinic cell carcinoma: 4/9 (44%) patients with AcCC achieved major response, and 5/9 (56%) had stable disease — 100% disease control rate. This is the first systemic therapy to show meaningful activity specifically in acinic cell carcinoma, a traditionally chemotherapy-refractory histology. However, data remains phase II level with small sample sizes.

survival_benefit.rationale: 44% ORR in acinic cell carcinoma is unprecedented for any systemic therapy in this histology. Overall median PFS for the full salivary gland cohort was not fully mature at data cutoff. Lenvatinib monotherapy showed 17.5-month median PFS in adenoid cystic carcinoma. The combination may offer meaningful disease control for recurrent/metastatic HGT-AcCC if surgery and RT fail.

accessibility.rationale: Currently available only through clinical trial participation (NCT04209660). Neither drug has FDA approval for salivary gland cancer indications. Both drugs are individually FDA-approved for other indications, potentially enabling off-label compassionate use in select cases, but insurance coverage would be uncertain.

safety_profile.rationale: Lenvatinib causes hypertension (40-60%), diarrhea, fatigue, decreased appetite, weight loss, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, proteinuria. Dose reductions needed in ~50% of patients. Pembrolizumab adds immune-related adverse events: thyroiditis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis. Wound complications were problematic with cabozantinib in irradiated fields — similar VEGFR-TKI class risk with lenvatinib in post-surgical/post-RT patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: Acinic cell carcinoma showed the best response among SGC subtypes in ASCO 2025 data (44% ORR vs lower rates in other histologies). No specific molecular biomarker is required. The PD-L1 status may predict pembrolizumab response but was not required in the trial. VEGFR pathway activation is common in salivary gland cancers, supporting lenvatinib mechanism.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenvatinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFR-alpha, RET, and KIT, blocking tumor angiogenesis and direct tumor cell signaling. VEGFR inhibition additionally modulates the tumor immune microenvironment by normalizing tumor vasculature and reducing immunosuppressive regulatory T cells, potentially synergizing with pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor) to restore antitumor T-cell immunity.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II study of lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab in recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancers (ASCO 2025)

biomarker_requirements: Acinic cell carcinoma histology showed best response rates
PD-L1 testing may be informative but not required
notable_side_effects: Wound healing complications in irradiated/surgical fields
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial (NCT04209660) — off-label use possible with both drugs FDA-approved for other indications

treatments.name: Immediate Facial Nerve Reconstruction with Nerve Grafting and Staged Reanimation

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple retrospective series support immediate nerve reconstruction at time of radical parotidectomy. JAMA Otolaryngology study (2018, n=285 total parotidectomy patients) showed 31.2% received concurrent facial reanimation. Masseteric nerve transfer data (Valencia-Sanchez 2024) and vascularized nerve flap data (Biglioli 2020) demonstrate functional recovery in 78% of patients. House-Brackmann grade III achieved in majority of successful cases.

survival_benefit.rationale: No direct impact on cancer survival or oncologic outcomes. However, quality of life is profoundly affected by facial paralysis, impacting ocular protection (corneal exposure), oral competence (drooling), speech, and psychosocial function. Patients with better facial function report significantly higher quality of life scores. Early reanimation may reduce the duration and severity of paralysis.

accessibility.rationale: Requires microsurgical expertise and specialized facial nerve reanimation team, typically available at academic medical centers and major head and neck surgery programs. Masseteric nerve transfer is increasingly accessible. Great auricular or sural nerve cable grafts are standard techniques. Cross-face nerve grafts and free muscle transfers require highest surgical expertise.

safety_profile.rationale: Additional operative time (1-3 hours) added to parotidectomy. Low morbidity from nerve harvest (sural nerve: lateral foot numbness; great auricular: ear lobule numbness). Masseteric nerve transfer avoids donor nerve morbidity. Does not compromise oncologic resection. Risk of graft failure without significant additional morbidity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent. Decision based on anatomic nerve sacrifice pattern, patient age (45 years old — favorable for nerve regeneration), prognosis (if expected survival >2 years, reanimation is justified), and patient preferences. Younger patients have better nerve regeneration capacity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Multiple reconstructive options: (1) Cable nerve grafting using sural or great auricular nerve interposition grafts to bridge defects in sacrificed facial nerve segments; (2) Masseteric nerve transfer — motor nerve to masseter is coapted to distal facial nerve stump, providing reliable neural input for smile restoration; (3) Cross-face nerve grafting from contralateral facial nerve for spontaneous smile; (4) Vascularized nerve flaps (thoracodorsal nerve) for long-gap reconstruction. Typically staged: immediate nerve repair at surgery, with planned secondary procedures (static slings, gold weight eyelid implants) if nerve regeneration is incomplete.

key_evidence.study_name: Analysis of Facial Reanimation Procedures Performed Concurrently With Total Parotidectomy and Facial Nerve Sacrifice

biomarker_requirements: No biomarker requirements — anatomic and functional assessment guides approach
notable_side_effects: Donor site morbidity (lateral foot numbness from sural nerve harvest)
Synkinesis (involuntary facial movements) with masseteric nerve transfer
Incomplete facial function recovery (House-Brackmann III-IV typical best outcome)
Potential need for secondary revision procedures
treatments.availability: Available at academic medical centers with microsurgical/facial reanimation expertise

treatments.name: Paclitaxel plus Carboplatin (TC Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II data showing 54.2% ORR in salivary gland cancers, superior to the CAP regimen (36.3%) in a comparative analysis. Limited sample sizes and no randomized head-to-head comparison. Increasingly favored over CAP due to better tolerability profile and comparable or superior response rates.

survival_benefit.rationale: 54.2% ORR is the highest reported for any chemotherapy doublet in salivary gland cancer. Median OS data similar to CAP regimen (~20-24 months). No cure potential in metastatic setting but provides meaningful disease control and symptom palliation. May be particularly useful for rapidly progressive HGT-AcCC requiring urgent tumor debulking.

accessibility.rationale: Both drugs are generic, widely available, and well-established in oncology practice. Carboplatin dosing based on AUC formula (renal function) makes it safer than cisplatin in many patients. Paclitaxel is a workhorse chemotherapy drug available at every oncology center.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than CAP: no doxorubicin cardiotoxicity, carboplatin less nephrotoxic and ototoxic than cisplatin. Paclitaxel causes peripheral neuropathy (dose-limiting), alopecia, and hypersensitivity reactions. Myelosuppression remains significant. Overall grade 3-4 toxicity rate 30-40%, lower than CAP.

biomarker_match.rationale: No predictive biomarkers for TC response in salivary gland cancer. Empiric activity across salivary gland cancer subtypes. High Ki-67 tumors may be more chemosensitive to taxane-platinum combinations.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Paclitaxel stabilizes microtubules, preventing their depolymerization and arresting cells in mitosis. Carboplatin forms intrastrand DNA cross-links, inhibiting DNA replication and transcription. The combination targets complementary cellular mechanisms: cytoskeletal disruption (paclitaxel) and DNA damage (carboplatin), with synergistic cytotoxicity in rapidly dividing cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Efficacy of cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and cisplatin versus paclitaxel and carboplatin in advanced salivary gland cancer (comparative analysis)

biomarker_requirements: Adequate renal function for carboplatin dosing (GFR >30 mL/min)
treatments.availability: Widely available — both drugs are generic and standard oncology agents

treatments.name: Concurrent Cisplatin Chemoradiation (Cisplatin 100 mg/m2 q3w with 66 Gy RT)

evidence_level.rationale: No randomized data specifically supports adding cisplatin to adjuvant RT in salivary gland cancer. A National Cancer Database analysis (Scientific Reports, 2022) found no survival benefit from adding chemotherapy to adjuvant radiation in advanced major salivary gland cancer. However, extrapolation from head and neck squamous cell carcinoma data and biological rationale for radiosensitization in high-grade tumors with adverse features supports consideration. NCCN lists chemoradiation as a category 2B option.

survival_benefit.rationale: No demonstrated OS advantage over RT alone in salivary gland cancer. However, for HGT-AcCC with extremely high recurrence rates (86%), the theoretical benefit of cisplatin radiosensitization may be warranted given the poor prognosis. Individual case reports have described responses to cisplatin-based chemoradiation in HGT-AcCC.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin is widely available and inexpensive as a generic chemotherapy agent. Concurrent chemoradiation protocols are standard practice in radiation oncology departments worldwide.

safety_profile.rationale: Adding cisplatin to radiation significantly increases toxicity: nephrotoxicity (requiring aggressive hydration), ototoxicity (particularly concerning as ipsilateral hearing may already be compromised), severe mucositis (grade 3 in 30-40%), nausea/emesis, myelosuppression, neurotoxicity. In the previously operated and potentially scarred field, wound healing concerns are amplified.

biomarker_match.rationale: No predictive biomarker for cisplatin response in salivary gland cancer. Ki-67 40% suggests rapidly proliferating tumor that may benefit from cytotoxic radiosensitization. High-grade transformation phenotype is the clinical rationale rather than a specific molecular target.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin acts as a radiosensitizer by forming platinum-DNA adducts that inhibit DNA repair, particularly homologous recombination repair of radiation-induced double-strand breaks. This potentiates the cytotoxic effect of concurrent radiation therapy. Administered at 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 22, and 43 of radiation, or alternatively weekly at 40 mg/m2 for improved tolerability.

key_evidence.study_name: No survival benefit from adding chemotherapy to adjuvant radiation in advanced major salivary gland cancer (NCDB analysis)

biomarker_requirements: Adequate renal function (GFR >50 mL/min) required for cisplatin
treatments.availability: Available at all oncology centers; cisplatin is generic and low-cost

treatments.name: Cyclophosphamide + Doxorubicin + Cisplatin (CAP Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II data from multiple studies (Airoldi 1996, n=22; Licitra 2011, n=not specified). No randomized phase III trials. The CAP regimen has been the most studied combination chemotherapy for advanced/recurrent salivary gland cancer, with consistent ORR of 27-36%. However, data is largely from the 1990s-2000s and not specific to HGT-AcCC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS of 21-23 months in mixed salivary gland cancer populations. ORR 27-36% with no complete responses in most series. For HGT-AcCC specifically, the PMC5446876 case report used cisplatin alone with radiation, with rapid distant failure despite treatment. Chemotherapy has not shown curative potential in salivary gland cancer but can provide disease control.

accessibility.rationale: All three drugs are generic, widely available, and low-cost. CAP regimen can be administered at any oncology center with standard chemotherapy infrastructure. Well-established dosing protocols and supportive care measures.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant cumulative toxicity: doxorubicin cardiotoxicity (cumulative dose limit 450-550 mg/m2), cisplatin nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity, cyclophosphamide hemorrhagic cystitis and myelosuppression. Combination regimen carries high grade 3-4 toxicity rate (40-60%). Febrile neutropenia risk requires G-CSF consideration. In a 45-year-old, doxorubicin cardiotoxicity is a particular long-term concern.

biomarker_match.rationale: No predictive biomarkers for CAP response in salivary gland cancer. High Ki-67 (40%) theoretically supports sensitivity to cytotoxic chemotherapy targeting rapidly dividing cells. NR4A3 rearrangement is not a predictive biomarker for chemotherapy response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Triple cytotoxic combination: cisplatin forms interstrand DNA cross-links; doxorubicin intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II; cyclophosphamide alkylates DNA, causing cross-links. The combination targets multiple DNA repair pathways simultaneously, maximizing cytotoxicity against rapidly proliferating high-grade tumor cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Cisplatin, doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide in advanced salivary gland carcinoma (Airoldi et al.)

biomarker_requirements: Adequate renal function (GFR >50 mL/min) for cisplatin
treatments.availability: Widely available — all components are generic chemotherapy drugs

treatments.name: Lenvatinib Monotherapy (Multi-Kinase Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II ACC-LEN14 trial data (n=32) in salivary gland cancers (predominantly adenoid cystic carcinoma) showed 15.6% ORR and 75% stable disease rate. Median PFS was 17.5 months. Centralized RECIST assessment confirmed durable disease stabilization. No specific acinic cell carcinoma data from monotherapy trials, but the ASCO 2025 combination data suggests lenvatinib has activity in this histology.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS of 17.5 months represents meaningful disease control, though few objective responses achieved. Disease stabilization rather than tumor shrinkage is the predominant pattern. For HGT-AcCC in the recurrent/metastatic setting, this PFS may exceed historical controls with conventional chemotherapy.

accessibility.rationale: Lenvatinib is FDA-approved for thyroid cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma, renal cell carcinoma, and endometrial carcinoma. Off-label use for salivary gland cancer requires physician advocacy. Oral administration is convenient. Cost approximately $15,000-20,000/month without insurance coverage.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ adverse events in 50% of patients requiring dose reductions. Hypertension, proteinuria, fatigue, diarrhea, and hand-foot syndrome are common. Wound complications were problematic with the related drug cabozantinib in previously irradiated salivary gland patients — similar risk exists with lenvatinib in this post-surgical, likely post-RT patient.

biomarker_match.rationale: No specific biomarker required. VEGFR pathway activation is common in salivary gland cancers. FGFR pathway may also contribute in some cases. The multi-kinase profile of lenvatinib provides broad target coverage. Acinic cell carcinoma specifically showed high response rates to lenvatinib+pembrolizumab combination at ASCO 2025.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenvatinib is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFR-alpha, RET, and KIT. It inhibits tumor angiogenesis (VEGFR), direct tumor cell proliferation (FGFR, RET), and stromal support (PDGFR). The anti-angiogenic mechanism is particularly relevant in salivary gland cancers, which are often highly vascularized.

key_evidence.study_name: ACC-LEN14: Lenvatinib in recurrent/metastatic adenoid cystic carcinoma of the salivary glands

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for other indications; off-label for salivary gland cancer

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy (Anti-PD-1 Immunotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1b KEYNOTE-028 data showed 12% ORR in PD-L1 positive salivary gland cancers. Notably, one responder had dedifferentiated acinic cell carcinoma, providing histology-specific evidence. Phase II KEYNOTE-158 data showed modest activity. Meta-analyses of pembrolizumab in salivary gland cancer confirm consistent but low response rates.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 3.8 months and median OS 13.0 months in KEYNOTE-028. Responses can be durable in responders, but the majority of patients do not respond. The dedifferentiated AcCC responder in KEYNOTE-028 suggests HGT may create neoantigens that enhance immunotherapy sensitivity. Disease control rate was 58%.

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab is FDA-approved for multiple solid tumor indications including MSI-H/dMMR tumors and TMB-H tumors (pan-tumor). Not specifically approved for salivary gland cancer. Off-label use may be considered; some insurance coverage possible under pan-tumor TMB-H approval if applicable. High cost (~$11,000/month).

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Immune-related adverse events include thyroiditis (10-15%), dermatitis, colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis (1-3% grade 3+). Most irAEs are manageable with immunosuppression. Fatal irAEs are rare (<1%). Quality of life preservation is superior to chemotherapy in most patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 expression, TMB, and MSI status should be tested. HGT-AcCC may have higher TMB than conventional AcCC due to accumulated genomic alterations during dedifferentiation. The TP53 mutations found in 40% of HGT cases may contribute to genomic instability and neoantigen generation. However, salivary gland cancers are generally low-TMB tumors.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody blocking PD-1 receptor on T cells, preventing engagement with PD-L1/PD-L2 ligands on tumor cells. This releases the immune checkpoint brake, allowing cytotoxic T cells to recognize and kill tumor cells. High-grade transformation may generate neoantigens through dedifferentiation-associated mutations, potentially increasing immunotherapy sensitivity.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-028: Pembrolizumab for advanced salivary gland carcinoma

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 positivity (CPS >= 1) was required in KEYNOTE-028
TMB-H (>=10 mut/Mb) enables pan-tumor FDA approval
MSI-H/dMMR status enables pan-tumor FDA approval
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for pan-tumor indications (MSI-H, TMB-H); off-label for salivary gland cancer

treatments.name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan (T-DXd, Enhertu) — if HER2-positive
treatments.category: Targeted Therapy — Investigational for Salivary Gland

evidence_level.rationale: MYTHOS phase II trial in HER2-positive salivary gland cancer showed impressive ORR of 68.4% (13/19) and DCR of 100%. Median PFS 15.9 months. However, all patients had salivary duct carcinoma, not acinic cell carcinoma. HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+) reported in 17% of HGT-AcCC cases (PMC4838973), making this potentially applicable in a subset.

survival_benefit.rationale: 68.4% ORR and 15.9-month median PFS are among the highest responses in salivary gland cancer trials. If HER2-positive by IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/ISH+, this represents a transformative treatment option. The DESTINY-PanTumor02 trial also showed activity in HER2-expressing solid tumors. However, applicability to HGT-AcCC depends on HER2 status testing.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for HER2-positive breast cancer, gastric cancer, and NSCLC. Pan-tumor approval for HER2-positive (IHC 3+) solid tumors based on DESTINY-PanTumor02. If HGT-AcCC tests HER2 IHC 3+, FDA-approved indication exists. Cost approximately $14,000/month.

safety_profile.rationale: Interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis occurred in 36.8% of MYTHOS trial patients (grade 1-2 in 6, grade 3 in 1). This is the key safety concern. Neutropenia (31.6% grade 3-4), lymphopenia, anemia also significant. Nausea and alopecia common. ILD risk requires vigilant monitoring with serial imaging.

biomarker_match.rationale: HER2 testing essential. HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+) found in only 17% of HGT-AcCC cases. HER2-low (IHC 1+ or 2+/ISH-) patients also eligible for T-DXd in some indications. NR4A3 rearrangement does not predict HER2 status. Must test the high-grade transformation component specifically.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Trastuzumab deruxtecan is an antibody-drug conjugate comprising an anti-HER2 antibody (trastuzumab) linked to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (deruxtecan/DXd) via a cleavable linker. Upon binding HER2 on tumor cells, the complex is internalized and the cytotoxic payload is released intracellularly, causing DNA damage and cell death. The bystander effect allows killing of nearby HER2-negative cells as released payload diffuses to adjacent cells.

key_evidence.study_name: MYTHOS: Phase II study of trastuzumab deruxtecan in HER2-positive recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancer

biomarker_requirements: HER2 overexpression in HGT-AcCC is uncommon (~17%)
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for HER2+ solid tumors (IHC 3+, pan-tumor); requires HER2 testing

treatments.name: Darolutamide plus Goserelin (Androgen Deprivation Therapy — if AR-positive)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial presented at ASCO 2025 (n=33) in AR-positive salivary gland cancers showed impressive ORR of 45.2%, clinical benefit rate of 51.6%, and median PFS of 13.1 months. However, predominantly tested in salivary duct carcinoma (an AR-positive histology). Acinic cell carcinoma is typically AR-negative, limiting applicability. AR testing required.

survival_benefit.rationale: If AR-positive (uncommon in acinic cell carcinoma), the combination showed median PFS of 13.1 months and 12-month OS rate of 87% — clinically meaningful. However, the likelihood of AR positivity in acinic cell carcinoma with HGT is low (<10-15%), making this treatment applicable only in the minority of patients testing positive.

accessibility.rationale: Darolutamide is FDA-approved for prostate cancer. Goserelin is widely available as a LHRH agonist. Off-label use would require AR testing and physician advocacy. Clinical trial enrollment possible. Cost significant but more manageable than targeted antibody therapies.

safety_profile.rationale: Favorable safety profile: grade 3 adverse events in only 18.2% of patients with no unexpected safety signals. Well-tolerated compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Main effects are androgen deprivation-related: hot flashes, fatigue, gynecomastia, musculoskeletal symptoms, metabolic changes. No significant overlapping toxicity with surgery or radiation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires androgen receptor positivity by immunohistochemistry. Acinic cell carcinoma is predominantly AR-negative. HGT component should be tested separately as dedifferentiation may alter receptor expression. If AR-positive, this is a strong match. If AR-negative (most likely), this treatment is not applicable.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Darolutamide is an androgen receptor antagonist that blocks testosterone binding to the AR, inhibiting AR-mediated transcription and tumor cell proliferation. Goserelin (LHRH agonist) suppresses gonadal testosterone production, creating a medical castration state. The combination achieves maximal androgen blockade, eliminating both circulating and intracellular androgen signaling in AR-dependent tumors.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II study of darolutamide and goserelin in AR-positive unresectable locally advanced or recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancer (ASCO 2025)

biomarker_requirements: Note: Acinic cell carcinoma is typically AR-negative; testing is essential
treatments.availability: Clinical trial; darolutamide FDA-approved for prostate cancer (off-label use possible)

treatments.name: Nivolumab plus Ipilimumab (Dual Checkpoint Inhibition)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial data (Fayette et al., Lancet Oncology 2023) in 64 patients with advanced salivary gland cancer. Non-ACC cohort (which would include HGT-AcCC) met primary efficacy endpoint with 16% ORR (5/32 patients). Responding tumors showed clonal T-cell expansion and mutational reduction, demonstrating biological mechanism of action.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS was 2.2 months in non-ACC cohort, reflecting that most patients did not respond. However, responders showed durable responses. OS data pending full maturity. The combination offers potential for durable disease control in the immunotherapy-responsive subset, but identifying predictive biomarkers remains challenging.

accessibility.rationale: Neither nivolumab nor ipilimumab is FDA-approved for salivary gland cancer. Both are approved for other solid tumors (melanoma, renal cell, etc.). Off-label combination use requires oncologist advocacy and insurance appeal. Combined cost exceeds $15,000/month. Clinical trial access may be available.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ treatment-related adverse events in 38% of patients (24/64). Dual checkpoint blockade carries higher toxicity than single-agent PD-1: increased rates of immune-related colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, and dermatitis. Requires close monitoring and prompt immunosuppression for grade 3+ irAEs.

biomarker_match.rationale: Non-ACC cohort (including potential HGT cases) showed better response than ACC cohort. Responding tumors harbored neoantigens including fusion-derived epitopes. HGT-AcCC with TP53 alterations (present in 40% of cases) and high Ki-67 may have higher neoantigen load. PD-L1, TMB testing recommended.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1) blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint while ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) blocks CTLA-4-mediated T-cell suppression. Dual checkpoint inhibition synergistically enhances anti-tumor immune responses by releasing both central (CTLA-4) and peripheral (PD-1) immune checkpoints. This combination promotes broader T-cell activation and may overcome resistance mechanisms seen with single-agent PD-1 blockade.

key_evidence.study_name: Nivolumab plus ipilimumab in advanced salivary gland cancer: a phase 2 trial (Fayette et al.)

biomarker_requirements: No mandatory biomarker for trial eligibility
PD-L1 expression, TMB, and MSI testing recommended
Non-ACC histology showed better response rates
treatments.availability: Clinical trial / Off-label use — both drugs FDA-approved for other indications

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab plus Vorinostat (Immunotherapy + Epigenetic Modifier)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II data from a single trial in recurrent/metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma and salivary gland cancer. Among 25 salivary gland cancer patients (3 with acinic cell carcinoma), ORR was 16% with median PFS of 4-6.9 months. Small sample size and mixed histology limit conclusions.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS of 13-14 months. The addition of vorinostat (HDAC inhibitor) to pembrolizumab aims to epigenetically reprogram tumor cells to increase immunogenicity. Theoretical advantage in HGT where epigenetic dysregulation may contribute to dedifferentiation. Limited data on durability of responses.

accessibility.rationale: Available primarily through clinical trial participation. Vorinostat is FDA-approved for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma only. Off-label combination use would require significant physician advocacy and likely be denied by most insurance payers. Cost of combination therapy is high.

safety_profile.rationale: Vorinostat adds fatigue, thrombocytopenia, nausea, and anorexia to pembrolizumab immune-related adverse events. Combined grade 3+ toxicity higher than pembrolizumab alone. However, the HDAC inhibitor toxicity is generally manageable and reversible.

biomarker_match.rationale: HDAC inhibition may upregulate MHC class I expression and tumor antigen presentation, potentially enhancing immunotherapy response. HGT-AcCC with epigenetic dysregulation during dedifferentiation may be particularly susceptible to HDAC inhibitor-mediated immunomodulation. No validated predictive biomarker.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vorinostat is a histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor that relaxes chromatin structure, upregulates tumor suppressor genes, increases MHC class I antigen presentation, and enhances tumor immunogenicity. When combined with pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1), the epigenetic priming by vorinostat may convert immunologically cold tumors into hot tumors susceptible to immune checkpoint blockade.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II trial of pembrolizumab and vorinostat in recurrent metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinomas and salivary gland cancer

biomarker_requirements: Tumor MHC class I expression may predict HDAC inhibitor benefit
treatments.availability: Clinical trial only; off-label combination use possible but not standard

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib and Pembrolizumab in Recurrent/Metastatic Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma and Salivary Gland Cancer
sites: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, New York, NY
biomarker_requirements: Histologically confirmed recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: ASCO 2025: 44% ORR in acinic cell carcinoma patients (4/9), 100% disease control rate. Median PFS pending full data maturity.

clinical_trials.title: Nivolumab plus Ipilimumab in Advanced Salivary Gland Cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: 16% ORR in non-ACC cohort (5/32 patients). Median PFS 2.2 months. Grade 3+ immune-related AEs in 38%. Responding tumors showed clonal T-cell expansion.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Darolutamide and Goserelin in AR-positive Salivary Gland Cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 45.2%, clinical benefit rate 51.6%, median PFS 13.1 months, 12-month OS rate 87%. Grade 3 AEs in 18.2%. Applicable only if HGT-AcCC tests AR-positive (uncommon).

clinical_trials.title: MYTHOS: Phase II Study of Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in HER2-positive Recurrent/Metastatic Salivary Gland Cancer
biomarker_requirements: HER2-positive (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/ISH+) or HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-)
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 68.4% (13/19), DCR 100%, median PFS 15.9 months. ILD/pneumonitis in 36.8% (mostly grade 1-2). All patients were salivary duct carcinoma; HER2 testing required for AcCC.

clinical_trials.title: ACC-LEN14: Lenvatinib in Recurrent/Metastatic Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma of the Salivary Glands
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 15.6% (5/32), DCR 90.6%, median PFS 17.5 months. Grade 3+ AEs in 50% requiring dose reductions. Disease stabilization was the predominant response pattern.

clinical_trials.title: Cabozantinib in Recurrent/Metastatic Salivary Gland Cancer
clinical_trials.status: Completed — closed early due to toxicity
clinical_trials.early_results: Closed prematurely after 25 patients due to severe wound complications (24% grade 3-5) especially in previously irradiated fields. ORR 7% in ACC, 25% in salivary duct carcinoma, 0% in other subtypes. Important cautionary data for VEGFR-TKI use in post-RT patients.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Adjuvant IMRT/Proton Therapy (60-66 Gy) with or without concurrent cisplatin
combination_strategies.rationale: Surgery plus adjuvant radiation is the established multimodal approach for T3N1 high-grade salivary gland cancer. RT addresses microscopic residual disease (positive margins found in 72% of HGT-AcCC resections). Concurrent cisplatin adds radiosensitization but without proven survival benefit in salivary gland cancer specifically. This combination is indicated for all high-risk features present in this case: T3 stage, N1 disease, high-grade transformation, and perineural invasion.

VEGFR inhibition by lenvatinib normalizes tumor vasculature, reduces immunosuppressive regulatory T cells, and promotes dendritic cell maturation, creating a more immunopermissive tumor microenvironment that synergizes with PD-1 checkpoint blockade. In acinic cell carcinoma specifically, this combination achieved 44% ORR and 100% DCR — the highest response rates reported for any systemic therapy in AcCC. This combination is the most promising systemic option for recurrent/metastatic HGT-AcCC after failure of local therapy.

HDAC inhibition by vorinostat epigenetically reprograms tumor cells to upregulate MHC class I expression and neoantigen presentation, potentially converting immunologically cold salivary gland tumors into hot tumors responsive to PD-1 checkpoint blockade. The epigenetic dysregulation inherent in high-grade transformation (dedifferentiation) may make HGT-AcCC particularly susceptible to HDAC inhibitor-mediated immune sensitization. Phase II ORR was 16% with median OS 14 months.

combination_strategies.base_therapy: Radical surgery with facial nerve sacrifice
combination_strategies.combination_partner: Immediate facial nerve reconstruction (masseteric nerve transfer or cable nerve grafting) + adjuvant radiation
combination_strategies.rationale: Performing facial nerve reconstruction simultaneously with oncologic resection optimizes rehabilitation outcomes by minimizing denervation time. Masseteric nerve transfer provides reliable motor input to distal facial nerve stumps and is not compromised by subsequent adjuvant radiation (the nerve transfer site is typically outside the high-dose radiation field). This integrated approach addresses both oncologic control and quality of life simultaneously, recognizing that facial function is a primary determinant of long-term quality of life in parotid cancer survivors.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Cyclophosphamide (CAP regimen) or substitution with Paclitaxel + Carboplatin (TC regimen)
combination_strategies.rationale: TC regimen showed higher ORR (54.2%) compared to CAP (36.3%) with potentially better tolerability (no doxorubicin cardiotoxicity). For a 45-year-old patient with potential for long-term survival, avoiding cumulative doxorubicin cardiotoxicity by choosing TC over CAP may be preferable. Both regimens are appropriate for palliative systemic therapy if local treatment options are exhausted.

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple retrospective series; Valencia-Sanchez 2024 (masseteric nerve transfer); JAMA Otolaryngology 2018 (n=285). Functional recovery achieved in 78% of patients with nerve reconstruction. House-Brackmann grade III (moderate dysfunction) achievable in 39% of selective reinnervation cases.
supportive_care.benefit: Restoration of facial tone, symmetry, and voluntary movement. Eye protection from corneal exposure (gold weight implant for incomplete eyelid closure). Oral competence restoration. Profound psychosocial and quality-of-life improvement. For this 45-year-old male, facial function restoration is critical for decades of remaining life.

supportive_care.evidence: ASCO supportive care guidelines; American Dental Association oncology protocols. Pre-RT dental evaluation reduces osteoradionecrosis risk from 3-7% to <1%. Fluoride trays, saliva substitutes, and pilocarpine/cevimeline improve quality of life post-radiation.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevention of radiation caries, osteoradionecrosis, and dental complications. Management of xerostomia from contralateral parotid and submandibular gland irradiation. This patient has already lost one parotid gland (prior surgery) — contralateral parotid preservation during radiation planning is critical to maintain any residual salivary function.

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple studies demonstrate that facial disfigurement from parotid cancer surgery significantly impacts body image, social functioning, depression, and anxiety. Cognitive behavioral therapy and peer support groups improve coping. National Head and Neck Cancer Alliance resources available.
supportive_care.benefit: Addresses depression, anxiety, social isolation, and body image disturbance resulting from facial paralysis, surgical scarring, and contour changes in a young adult male. Early psychological intervention improves treatment compliance and overall quality of life.

supportive_care.approach: Physical Therapy for Shoulder and Neck Rehabilitation Post-Neck Dissection
supportive_care.evidence: Systematic reviews demonstrate that early physical therapy after neck dissection reduces shoulder disability, improves range of motion, and decreases chronic pain. Modified radical neck dissection preserving the accessory nerve reduces but does not eliminate shoulder dysfunction.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevention of frozen shoulder, restoration of shoulder abduction and external rotation, pain management, lymphedema prevention through manual lymphatic drainage. Important for functional independence and return to work in a 45-year-old.

supportive_care.evidence: MASCC/ESPEN guidelines for head and neck cancer patients undergoing radiation therapy. Speech-language pathology swallowing assessment reduces aspiration risk. Dietitian-directed nutritional optimization improves treatment tolerance and outcomes.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains nutritional status during concurrent chemoradiation. Swallowing exercises before and during RT reduce severity of radiation-induced dysphagia. Prophylactic gastrostomy tube placement may be considered if significant weight loss anticipated. Trismus prevention exercises if jaw muscles in radiation field.

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN recommends comprehensive genomic profiling for advanced/recurrent salivary gland cancers to identify actionable alterations. HER2 overexpression (17% HGT-AcCC), AR expression, TMB-H, MSI-H, NTRK fusions are actionable. NR4A3 rearrangement confirmation supports diagnosis.
supportive_care.benefit: Identifies potential targeted therapy options including trastuzumab deruxtecan (if HER2+), androgen deprivation (if AR+), checkpoint inhibitors (if TMB-H/MSI-H), and TRK inhibitors (if NTRK fusion+). May reveal unexpected actionable alterations in the high-grade transformation component. Essential for treatment planning in recurrent/metastatic setting.
